meta.title: SQL & Database Migrations
meta.description: Review checklist for SQL queries, schema changes, and database migrations

items.text: Do table and column names follow consistent naming conventions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tables and columns should use consistent naming: snake_case or camelCase, singular or plural table names, and descriptive column names.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inconsistent naming makes queries harder to write and causes confusion about which table or column to use.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that new tables and columns follow the existing project conventions. Look for abbreviations, inconsistent pluralization, or unclear names.
baseContent.exampleComment: This table uses camelCase but the rest of the schema uses snake_case. Please rename to match conventions.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Follow the established naming conventions. Consistency matters more than any specific style.

items.text: Are column data types appropriate for the data being stored?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Columns should use the most appropriate type: UUID for IDs, TIMESTAMPTZ for timestamps, NUMERIC for money, TEXT vs VARCHAR, BOOLEAN instead of integer flags.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong data types waste storage, lose precision (especially with money/timestamps), or prevent proper validation at the database level.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check each column type against the data it will store. Watch for VARCHAR(255) used as a default, FLOAT for money, or TIMESTAMP without timezone.
baseContent.exampleComment: This price column uses FLOAT. Use NUMERIC(10,2) instead to avoid floating-point precision errors with currency.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Choose data types deliberately. The database should enforce data integrity at the type level.

items.text: Do all tables have appropriate primary keys?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every table should have a primary key. Consider UUID vs serial/BIGSERIAL vs natural keys based on your use case.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing primary keys prevent proper row identification, break ORM usage, and make joins unreliable.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that each new table defines a PRIMARY KEY. Verify the key type matches the project standard (UUID, serial, etc.).
baseContent.exampleComment: This table has no primary key defined. Add an id column or define a composite primary key.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every table needs a primary key. Use UUIDs for distributed systems, serial for simple applications.

items.text: Are foreign key constraints defined for relationships?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Columns referencing other tables should have FOREIGN KEY constraints with appropriate ON DELETE/ON UPDATE actions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without foreign keys, the database cannot enforce referential integrity, leading to orphaned rows and inconsistent data.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check columns that reference other tables (user_id, order_id, etc.). Verify they have REFERENCES constraints and appropriate cascade behavior.
baseContent.exampleComment: user_id references users(id) but has no ON DELETE action. Add ON DELETE CASCADE or ON DELETE SET NULL depending on requirements.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Define foreign keys for all relationships. Specify ON DELETE behavior explicitly.

items.text: Are NOT NULL constraints used where appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Columns that should always have a value should be marked NOT NULL. Default to NOT NULL and only allow NULL when it has a meaningful semantic purpose.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Nullable columns complicate queries (three-value logic), create bugs when NULL is unexpected, and make application code more complex.
baseContent.howToVerify: Review each new column. If it should always have a value, ensure it has NOT NULL. Check for columns that could use DEFAULT values instead of allowing NULL.
baseContent.exampleComment: created_at should be NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(). There's no valid case for a row without a creation timestamp.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Default to NOT NULL. Only allow NULL when absence of a value is semantically meaningful.

items.text: Is the schema appropriately normalized (or intentionally denormalized)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tables should generally be in 3NF. Denormalization should be intentional and documented, usually for performance reasons.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Under-normalized schemas lead to update anomalies and inconsistent data. Over-normalized schemas cause excessive joins and poor performance.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for repeated data across tables, composite columns that should be split, or excessive normalization that requires many joins for simple queries.
baseContent.exampleComment: The user's email is stored in both the users table and the orders table. Consider normalizing to avoid stale data.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Normalize by default. Denormalize intentionally with a documented reason.

items.text: Is the migration reversible with a down/rollback?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Migrations should include a rollback (down) migration that undoes the change. Destructive migrations should be carefully considered.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without rollback capability, a failed deployment may leave the database in a broken state with no easy recovery path.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for a corresponding down migration. Verify it actually reverses the up migration correctly.
baseContent.exampleComment: This migration adds a column but has no down migration. Add ALTER TABLE ... DROP COLUMN in the rollback.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always write a down migration. If the change is truly irreversible, document why.

items.text: Is the migration backwards-compatible with the current running code?

baseContent.whatItMeans: During deployment, old code runs against the new schema. Migrations must not break queries from the currently deployed version.
baseContent.whyItMatters: In zero-downtime deployments, there is a window where old code runs against the new schema. Breaking changes cause outages.
baseContent.howToVerify: Consider what happens if the current production code runs against this new schema. Will existing SELECTs, INSERTs, UPDATEs still work?
baseContent.exampleComment: Renaming this column will break the current production code. Use a multi-step migration: add new column, backfill, deploy code, drop old column.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Migrations must be compatible with the currently deployed code. Use expand-contract for breaking changes.

items.text: Will the migration avoid locking tables for extended periods?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Some DDL operations acquire exclusive locks that block all reads and writes. On large tables, this can cause minutes of downtime.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A locked table means the application cannot read or write that data, causing request timeouts and potential cascading failures.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for ALTER TABLE on large tables. Adding a column with a DEFAULT, creating an index without CONCURRENTLY, or changing a column type can all lock the table.
baseContent.exampleComment: CREATE INDEX on a 50M row table will lock it. Use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use CONCURRENTLY for indexes on large tables. Avoid adding columns with defaults on large tables in older PostgreSQL versions.

items.text: Are data migrations separated from schema migrations?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Schema changes (DDL) and data changes (DML) should be in separate migrations. Data migrations should be idempotent.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Mixing DDL and DML in one transaction can cause long locks. Separate migrations are easier to debug, rollback, and retry.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the migration mixes CREATE/ALTER TABLE with UPDATE/INSERT statements. These should be separate migration files.
baseContent.exampleComment: This migration adds a column and backfills data in the same step. Split into two migrations: one for schema, one for data.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep schema and data migrations separate. Data migrations should be idempotent and safe to re-run.

items.text: Is the migration safe to run multiple times?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Migrations should use IF NOT EXISTS for creates, IF EXISTS for drops, and ON CONFLICT for data changes where possible.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Migration tools sometimes need to re-run migrations. Non-idempotent migrations fail on retry, leaving the database in an unclear state.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for CREATE TABLE without IF NOT EXISTS, CREATE INDEX without IF NOT EXISTS, or INSERT without ON CONFLICT.
baseContent.exampleComment: Use CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS to make this migration safe to re-run.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use IF NOT EXISTS and ON CONFLICT to make migrations idempotent where possible.

items.text: Are appropriate indexes defined for query patterns?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Columns used in WHERE, JOIN, and ORDER BY clauses should have indexes. Composite indexes should match query patterns.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing indexes cause full table scans that grow linearly with data. A query that takes 10ms on 1K rows takes 10s on 1M rows.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check each new query for its WHERE and JOIN conditions. Verify matching indexes exist. Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to confirm.
baseContent.exampleComment: This query filters by status and created_at but there's no composite index. Add CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status_created ON orders(status, created_at).
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Create indexes to match your query patterns. Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to verify they are used.

items.text: Are N+1 query patterns avoided?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Loading a list of items and then querying for each item's related data individually creates N+1 queries. Use JOINs or batch loading instead.
baseContent.whyItMatters: N+1 queries are the most common performance problem. Loading 100 items with N+1 makes 101 database round-trips instead of 1-2.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for queries inside loops. Check ORM usage for lazy loading patterns. Watch for .map() or .forEach() that trigger individual queries.
baseContent.exampleComment: This loads each user's orders in a loop. Use a JOIN or WHERE user_id IN (...) to batch load instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never query inside a loop. Use JOINs, subqueries, or batch loading.

items.text: Are all queries parameterized (no string concatenation)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: SQL queries must use parameterized queries or prepared statements. Never concatenate user input into SQL strings.
baseContent.whyItMatters: SQL injection is one of the most dangerous and common vulnerabilities. It can lead to complete database compromise.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for string concatenation or template literals building SQL queries. Check for f-strings, format(), or + operators with SQL.
baseContent.exampleComment: This builds SQL with string concatenation: `WHERE name = '${name}'`. Use parameterized queries: `WHERE name = $1` with parameters.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use parameterized queries. Never concatenate user input into SQL.

items.text: Do queries SELECT specific columns instead of SELECT *?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Queries should select only the columns they need rather than using SELECT *.
baseContent.whyItMatters: SELECT * fetches unnecessary data, breaks when columns are added/removed, and prevents covering index optimizations.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for SELECT * in production queries (not ad-hoc debugging). Check ORM queries for equivalent patterns.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses SELECT * but only needs name and email. Select only the needed columns.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Select only the columns you need. It improves performance and makes intent clear.

items.text: Do list queries use proper pagination?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Queries that return variable-length results should use LIMIT/OFFSET or cursor-based pagination to prevent unbounded result sets.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without pagination, a query can return millions of rows, exhausting memory and causing timeouts or crashes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for queries without LIMIT that could return many rows. Verify pagination is implemented correctly (OFFSET-based or cursor-based).
baseContent.exampleComment: This query has no LIMIT. If there are 1M matching rows, this will exhaust memory. Add pagination.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always paginate list queries. Prefer cursor-based pagination for large datasets.

items.text: Are related operations wrapped in transactions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Operations that must succeed or fail together should be in a database transaction with appropriate isolation level.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without transactions, a failure partway through leaves data in an inconsistent state (e.g., money deducted but not credited).
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for multi-step operations that modify data. Verify they use BEGIN/COMMIT or the ORM's transaction API.
baseContent.exampleComment: The balance deduction and credit are separate queries without a transaction. Wrap them in a transaction to prevent inconsistency.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use transactions for operations that must be atomic. Choose the right isolation level for your use case.

items.text: Are database permissions properly scoped?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Application database users should have minimum necessary permissions. Don't use superuser or admin roles for application connections.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An overprivileged database user turns a SQL injection vulnerability into a complete database compromise including schema changes and data export.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check what database user the application connects as. Verify it doesn't have unnecessary permissions like DROP, CREATE, or SUPERUSER.
baseContent.exampleComment: The application connects as the postgres superuser. Create a dedicated role with only SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE on needed tables.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Apply least privilege. Application users should only have permissions they actually need.

items.text: Is sensitive data properly handled (encryption, hashing)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Passwords must be hashed (bcrypt/argon2). PII and sensitive fields should be encrypted at rest. Consider column-level encryption for highly sensitive data.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A database breach exposes all stored data. Proper hashing and encryption limit the damage.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for columns storing passwords, SSNs, credit cards, or other sensitive data. Verify they are hashed or encrypted, not stored in plaintext.
baseContent.exampleComment: This stores the API key in plaintext. Hash it or encrypt it at rest.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Hash passwords, encrypt sensitive data. Never store secrets in plaintext.

items.text: Is row-level access control enforced where needed?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Multi-tenant applications should enforce tenant isolation at the database level using RLS policies or always including tenant_id in queries.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing tenant isolation allows one user to access another user's data. This is a critical security vulnerability in multi-tenant systems.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check queries on shared tables for tenant_id or user_id filters. Verify RLS policies are enabled if using PostgreSQL RLS.
baseContent.exampleComment: This query on the orders table doesn't filter by tenant_id. A user could see other tenants' orders.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always enforce tenant isolation. Use RLS or application-level filtering on every query.

items.text: Have expensive queries been analyzed with EXPLAIN?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Complex queries should be tested with EXPLAIN ANALYZE to verify the query plan uses indexes and doesn't do full table scans.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A query that looks simple may hide sequential scans, hash joins on large tables, or suboptimal plans that degrade with data growth.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on new queries with representative data. Check for Seq Scan on large tables and high estimated row counts.
baseContent.exampleComment: Please run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on this query with production-like data and share the output. I suspect a sequential scan.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE for any query touching large tables or used in hot paths.

items.text: Is connection pooling used appropriately?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Applications should use connection pools (PgBouncer, application-level pools) rather than opening new connections per request.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Database connections are expensive to create. Without pooling, high traffic causes connection exhaustion and the database rejects new connections.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the database connection configuration. Verify pool size limits are set and appropriate for the deployment.
baseContent.exampleComment: This creates a new connection per request. Use a connection pool with a max size matching your database's max_connections.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use connection pooling. Size pools based on your database's max_connections.

items.text: Are operations on large tables done in batches?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Bulk updates, deletes, and data migrations on large tables should be done in batches to avoid long-running transactions and lock contention.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A single UPDATE on 10M rows creates a massive transaction that holds locks, bloats WAL, and may cause replication lag or OOM.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for UPDATE or DELETE without a LIMIT on tables with many rows. Look for data backfill scripts that process all rows at once.
baseContent.exampleComment: This UPDATE affects all rows in a 5M row table. Process in batches of 10,000 with a small delay between batches.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Batch large data operations. Never update millions of rows in a single transaction.

items.text: Are there unnecessary or duplicate indexes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each index adds write overhead and storage cost. Duplicate indexes (where one index is a prefix of another) or unused indexes should be removed.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Too many indexes slow down writes significantly. Each INSERT/UPDATE must update every index on the table.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for indexes that overlap (e.g., idx(a) and idx(a,b) — the latter covers both). Check pg_stat_user_indexes for unused indexes.
baseContent.exampleComment: There's already an index on (user_id, created_at). The new index on just (user_id) is redundant.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every index has a cost. Only add indexes that serve actual query patterns.

items.text: Has the migration been tested against a copy of production data?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Migrations should be tested against a database with production-like data volume and schema to catch issues that don't appear on empty databases.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A migration that runs in 1 second on a test database may take 30 minutes on production, causing downtime.
baseContent.howToVerify: Ask if the migration was tested on a staging database with production-scale data. Check for timing results.
baseContent.exampleComment: Has this migration been tested against a staging database? The orders table has 5M rows — this ALTER could be slow.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always test migrations against production-scale data. Empty databases hide performance issues.

items.text: Is seed or test data provided for new tables?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New tables should have corresponding seed data or factory definitions for testing and local development.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without seed data, other developers cannot test features that depend on the new tables without manually inserting data.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for seed files, factory definitions, or fixture data that populate the new tables.
baseContent.exampleComment: This adds a new categories table but no seed data. Please add seed data for local development.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Provide seed data for new tables. It enables testing and improves developer experience.

items.text: Are breaking schema changes documented?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Schema changes that affect other teams or services should be documented in the PR description with migration instructions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Other services may query the same database. Undocumented schema changes can silently break other teams' code.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the PR description for documentation of breaking changes. Verify affected services or teams have been notified.
baseContent.exampleComment: This drops a column used by the analytics service. Please document the breaking change and coordinate with that team.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Document breaking schema changes. Coordinate with all consumers of the affected tables.

items.text: Is there a rollback plan for the migration?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Migrations that modify or delete data should have a documented rollback plan including data backup, migration reversal, and verification steps.
baseContent.whyItMatters: If a migration goes wrong in production, a clear rollback plan is the difference between a 5-minute fix and a multi-hour incident.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for a rollback plan in the PR description. For destructive migrations, verify that data backup steps are included.
baseContent.exampleComment: This drops a column with data. What's the rollback plan if we need that data back? Consider backing up to a temp table first.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Have a rollback plan before running migrations in production. Back up data before destructive changes.
